coredata.dc:title: The electrochemical detection of prostate specific antigen on glassy carbon electrode modified with combinations of graphene quantum dots, cobalt phthalocyanine and an aptamer
coredata.dc:creator: Nxele, Siphesihle Robin
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel aptasensor is developed for the electrochemical detection of prostate specific antigen (PSA) on electrode surfaces modified using various combinations of a Cobalt phthalocyanine (CoPc), an aptamer and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) as well as differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) are employed for the detection of PSA. In both analytical techniques, linear calibration curves were observed at a concentration range of 1.2–2.0 pM. The glassy carbon electrode where CoPc and GQDs are placed on the electrode when non-covalently linked followed by addition of the aptamer (GQDs-CoPc(ππ)-aptamer (sequential)) showed the best performance with a limit of detection (LoD) as low as 0.66 pM when using DPV. The detection limits were much lower than the dangerous levels reported for PSA in males tested for prostate cancer. This electrode showed selectivity for PSA in the presence of bovine serum albumin, glucose and L-cysteine. The aptasensor showed good stability, reproducibility and repeatability, deeming it a promising early detection device for prostate cancer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Prostate specific antigen
Aptamer
Graphene quantum dots
Cobalt phthalocyanine
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
Differential pulse voltammetry

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst otherkwds primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrode and solution preparation for detection of PSA
Verification of covalently linked conjugates using XPS
Charge transfer using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)
Concentration studies using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS survey and high-resolution scans for (a) GQDs@Aptamer (7) and (b) CoPc@Aptamer (11) for the verification of covalent bonding with (i) Wide survey scans and (ii) N1s, (iii) C1s and (iv) O1s deconvoluted high resolution scans.
Electrodes in 0.1 M KCl containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution. Scan rate = 100 mV/s. (Starting potential of scans = −1.0 V).

Nyquist plots of modified electrodes in a 2 μM PSA solution in 30 mM phosphate buffer solution of pH 7.4. Plots separated into three groups for ease of comparison. Circuit fitting model shown in Fig. S3.

(a) Differential pulse voltammograms (DPV) and (b) electrochemical impedance (EIS) Nyquist plots of GCE-GQDs-CoPc(ππ)-Aptamer (sequential) in a 30 mM phosphate buffer solution of pH 7.4 with PSA concentrations ranging from 1.2–2.0 pM. Inserts: Linear responses of currents and resistance to changing concentrations where the red line in the insert for (a) is for the peaks near 0 V and the blue line is for the peaks near - 0.65 V. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Differential pulse voltammograms of (a) GCE-GQDs-CoPc-Aptamer, in a 30 mM phosphate buffer solution of pH 7.4 with PSA concentration of 2.0 pM. Linear plots of (b) Peak current (IP) vs ν and (c) Peak current (IP) vs ν−2.

Differential pulse voltammograms of GCE-GQDs-CoPc(ππ)-Aptamer (16) (sequential) in the absence and presence of interferents (2 nm of BSA, glucose, and L-cysteine) in a PSA solution of concentration 2.0 pM.
The ππ conjugation of CoPc with GQDs to form GQDs-CoPc (π-π) (5).

The list of electrode modifiers used. The schematic representations are shown in Table S1 (illustrations are not accurate representations of physical characteristics but only an idea of how they would interact).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: @ represents covalent linking, ππ = non-covalent linking, premixed = electrodes modified by a combination of the components of the conjugates without first forming a bond, sequential = electrode modification by each component sequential. In the case of sequential modification, the first modifier is the one shown close to the GCE.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summarized cyclic voltammetry data for all electrodes.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aFor sequential electrodes, the modifiers are placed in the order in which they were used to modify the electrode, with the first modifier place near GCE.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of determined RCT and kapp values determined from Nyquist plots (in order of increasing Rct values).
Summary of limits of detection (LoD) of selected electrode surfaces determined from EIS and DPV measurements (Linear detection range: 1.2–2pM).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ⁎Values in brackets are the potentials (V) at peaks are identified to determine LoD.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of results obtained in this work for GCE-GQDs-CoPc (ππ)-Aptamer (sequential) compared to electrochemical PSA aptasensors reported in literature (units used in this work converted from pM to pg/mL for ease of comparison).

head.ce:title: The electrochemical detection of prostate specific antigen on glassy carbon electrode modified with combinations of graphene quantum dots, cobalt phthalocyanine and an aptamer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Chemistry Department, Rhodes University, Grahamstown 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Chemistry Department, Rhodes University, Grahamstown, 6140 South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel aptasensor is developed for the electrochemical detection of prostate specific antigen (PSA) on electrode surfaces modified using various combinations of a Cobalt phthalocyanine (CoPc), an aptamer and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) as well as differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) are employed for the detection of PSA. In both analytical techniques, linear calibration curves were observed at a concentration range of 1.2–2.0 pM. The glassy carbon electrode where CoPc and GQDs are placed on the electrode when non-covalently linked followed by addition of the aptamer (GQDs-CoPc(ππ)-aptamer (sequential)) showed the best performance with a limit of detection (LoD) as low as 0.66 pM when using DPV. The detection limits were much lower than the dangerous levels reported for PSA in males tested for prostate cancer. This electrode showed selectivity for PSA in the presence of bovine serum albumin, glucose and L-cysteine. The aptasensor showed good stability, reproducibility and repeatability, deeming it a promising early detection device for prostate cancer.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the most common cancers that cause the death of males on a local and global scale is prostate cancer [1]. The symptoms related to prostate cancer often present themselves at the advanced stages, which then requires more aggressive treatment. For this reason, it is important to develop devices that will allow for early detection which would then hopefully prevent the spread of the disease, allowing for quicker recovery of patients after treatment. According to recent reports [2], the Digital Rectal Examination (DRE) and Prostate-Specific Antigen (PSA) blood tests are run to determine whether a patient has prostate cancer or not, with the PSA test being more sensitive and a better predictor of prostate cancer [3]. PSA has also been detected using fluorescent labelling [4], surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [5] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [6], TiO2 silk nanofiber composite [7], just to name a few. In this work we employ PSA aptamer in the presence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and a Co phthalocyanine (CoPc) for the first time.

Several immunosensors have been fabricated for the detection of biomarkers using antibodies as the biorecognition element [8–11]. However, the immobilization of antibodies has been reported to decrease reaction activity thus leading to the increasing demand of the use of aptamers which are single stranded DNA or RNA, also known as “artificial antibodies” [12–14]. Aptamers have proven to have high sensitivity, selectivity and stability [15,16]. The aptamer used in this work, specifically binds to PSA.

Herein, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a push/pull Co(II) phthalocyanine (CoPc) and the PSA aptamer are used, for the first time (to our knowledge) in combination to fabricate a biosensor for the early detection of prostate cancer. The CoPc has substituents (tris-tert-butyl) that act as electron donating groups (push) and the single COOH as an electron withdrawing group (pull), Scheme 1 . The role of the CoPc in this work is to enhance electron transfer. Pc molecules are known to have good electronic properties [17]. The COOH group's main role will be to covalently link the CoPc to the aptamer. The GQDs also possess COOH groups which will be used for the same purpose. In addition to the role of the functional groups, GQDs, as with other graphene-based nanostructures, have been proven to improve conductivity [18,19], increase surface area [20], provide stability [21] and retain the bioactivity of the biomolecules they are linked to [19]. Although graphene oxide (GO) has been given attention in biosensor work, it has been found to be unstable due to the aggressive oxidation and a high frequency ultrasonic exfoliation, which results in the destruction of the hexagonal lattice of the graphene matrix [22], which essentially gives graphene its robustness. For this reason, GQDs then may be viewed as superior for this application due to their high stability and their ease of synthesis. The aptamer used in this work affords the fabricated immunosensor its selectivity and specificity as it is designed to bind to PSA. The effect of linking (as opposed to mixing with) the aptamer to CoPc and GQDs will also be investigated in order to study its effects on the detection of PSA.

ZnPc derivatives have been linked to an aptamer for human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) detection [23] and for biosensing [24]. GQDs have also been linked to an aptamer in the presence of a porphyrin (with related structure to phthalocyanines) for photothermal and photodynamic therapy [25]. A metallophthalocyanine (MPc) has been employed for the photoelectrochemical detection of PSA in the presence of graphene [26]. GQDs have also been employed for electrochemiluminescence detection of PSA [27]. In this work we employ differential pulse voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy for the electrochemical characterization and detection of PSA. This is the first time an MPc or GQDs are linked to an aptamer for the detection of PSA and it is the first time where all three (MPc, aptamer and GQDs) are employed together for sensing. Although the MPc and GQDs both have COOH groups to covalently link to aptamer, their use in combination as opposed to choosing one over the other is to improve the electrocatalytic ability.

Ethylenediaminotetraacetic acid (EDTA), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), and prostate specific antigen (PSA) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich. [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution (1 mM in 0.1 M KCl) was used for the characterization of the modified electrodes in cyclic voltammetry. A phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution of pH 7.4, 30 mM, was employed in this work. The pH of PBS was adjusted using 0.1 M HCl or NaOH where necessary. Tris-HCl (10 mM, pH 8) was employed in this work for the storage of the aptamer in solution as advised by supplier. The synthesis of the push pull Co(II) phthalocyanine has been reported in literature [28]. The synthesis of GQDs has also been reported in literature [29]. The amine-functionalized PSA-specific aptamer was synthesized by (and purchased from) Integrated DNA technologies (IDT) and purified using high-performance liquid chromatography and its sequences is listed as follows: single-stranded DNA (ssDNA): 5′-NH2-(CH2)6-TTT TTA ATT AAA GCT CGC CAT CAA ATA GCT TT-3′ (235 nmol, >90% purity). Type II water which was obtained from an Elga PURELAB Chorus 2 (RO/DI) system.
All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received from commercial suppliers.

The instrumentation used as well as preparation of conjugates may be found in the Supporting Information. A complete list of different combinations of electrode modifiers and their representation is presented in Table 1 and Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrode and solution preparation for detection of PSA
ce:section.ce:para: Regarding the detection of PSA using EIS and DPV, the modified electrodes were first incubated for 2 h in a 2 μM PSA solution in 30 mM phosphate buffer solution of pH 7.4 at room temperature after which the impedance measurements were performed. For concentration studies using EIS and DPV, the stock solution was further diluted to produce samples with concentrations ranging from 1.2 to 2.0 pM. The electrodes used for concentration studies were also incubated in each solution prior to EIS and DPV measurements being recorded.

The structure of the CoPc derivative employed in this work is shown in Scheme 1 together with ππ conjugation to GQDs. Covalent linking to form an amide bond between COOH functional groups of the CoPc or GQDs with the NH2 of the aptamer, was achieved by carbodiimide coupling chemistry and the method derived from literature reports [30,31].

The aptamer structure has primary amines in guanine, cytosine and adenine residues, but because of the folding, these primary amine groups are not readily available for binding with other functional groups. Hence why 5′ end is functionalized as it Is accessible with no bulky groups hindering reactivity [32]. In literature [32], the 5′ end is the one functionalized for further conjugation of the aptamer to other molecules and this article includes a basic schematic of the aptamer and how it binds to a biomarker. It is clear in this illustration that binding could only be possible via 5′ end or 3′ end.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of covalently linked conjugates using XPS
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the success of covalent linking of the aptamer with the GQDs and the CoPc, XPS analysis was employed. High resolution scans in the N1s (ii), C1s (iii) and O1s (iv) were deconvoluted and analyzed to confirm the presence of expected bonds as shown in Fig. 1 for (A) GQDs@Aptamer (7) and (B) CoPc@Aptamer (11) (‘@’ representing covalent linking of molecules).

The wide survey scans (represented as ‘(i)’) showed the expected peaks which were attributable to GQDs@Aptamer and CoPc@Aptamer. The observed peaks on the wide scan were the N1s at 400 eV, C1s at 300 eV, O1s around 550 eV, P2s at 197 eV and P2p around 150 eV both wide scans. The difference in the survey scans was just the peak due to cobalt which is the central metal of the phthalocyanine used, observed at around 791 eV which is the region where Co is observed on XPS analyses [33,34]. The P is reported to be due to the DNA phosphate group [35]. P has been observed at binding energies of 150–200 eV [36].

The peaks due to nitrogen, N1s (ii), carbon, C1s (iii) and oxygen, O1s (iv) were further studied as high-resolution scans and deconvoluted in order to confirm the covalent conjugation of the aptamer with GQDs and CoPc. For the GQDs@Aptamer, the N1s spectrum showed 2 main peaks underneath the original peak at binding energies of 399.0 eV and 401.0 eV which are attributable to NH and NCO respectively and these bonds at these binding energies have been reported [37]. The C1s spectrum showed 3 peaks after deconvolution at binding energies of 283.5 eV, 285 eV and 287 eV attributable to graphitic carbon due to the GQDs, CN and CO respectively. The O1s spectrum showed 3 peaks under the main peak at binding energies of 529.0 eV, 531.0 eV and 531.8 eV which may be attributed to COC/CO, CO/PO and COP respectively. A similar finding for aptamer-based work has been reported in literature [35]. For the CoPc@Aptamer conjugate, the deconvoluted N1s spectrum showed peaks at binding energies of 397 eV, 399 eV and 401 eV which may be attributed to CN, NH and NCO respectively [37]. For the C1s and O1s spectra, similar results were observed as was for GQDs@Aptamer which confirms the presence of the new amide bond formed by covalent linkage as well as presence of NCO bond [38].
The list of electrodes employed is shown in Table 1 with the representation in Table S1 (supporting Information). Cyclic voltammetry was used to characterize the modified electrode surfaces.

The modified electrode surfaces were tested in the presence of Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox couple, using cyclic voltammetry. Some of the cyclic voltammograms are shown in Fig. 2 where electrodes are tested in a 0.1 M KCl solution containing 1 mM ferricyanide (pH 7.5). Fig. 2 is an expanded version of Fig. S1 (Supporting Information) to make the identified peaks more visible. Peaks labelled (ii) are due to [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox couple. Peaks near −0.5 V (i) are due to electrode modifiers associated with the redox processes of GQDs and have been observed before for modified electrodes containing GQDs [39]. The presence of oxygen rich groups at the GQDs surface may result in their oxidation making GQDs good redox mediators. The tunable size of GQDs allows them to act as multivalent redox species which are observed in cyclic voltammetry. Peaks in this region for electrodes containing CoPc could be due to the CoIIPc−2/CoIPc−2 redox couple [40]. The DNA also shows the peaks in this region [41], hence a peak in the region is observed for the aptamer alone (electrode 4). Peaks at 0.70 V (III) could be due to ring based process in electrodes containing CoPc [40]. However, the oxidation of amino acids such as guanine which constitute the aptamer also occur in this region [42]. The electrode (4) containing aptamer alone also shows this peak, though very weak.

Peak current and peak separation (ΔEp) are recorded (Table 2 ) which gives us some information on the nature of the type of surface we are working with. The lower ΔEp is related to better charge transfer process. Additionally, peak currents are influenced by the modified regions where fast electron transfer occur, as well as the unmodified regions where slow electron transfer occur leading to peak broadening and widening of peak potential separation [43]. Firstly, the ΔEp of the GCE-GQDs increases quite drastically compared to the bare GCE which is unexpected as it is assumed that the GQDs will enhance electron transfer ability of the electrode. However, it is possible for there to be repulsion between the oxygen-containing groups on the GQDs and the anions of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4-.

The largest ΔEp is observed for GCE-GQDs@Aptamer (617 mV). Comparing the different combinations of electrodes using GQDs and aptamer, the increase in ΔEp is as follows: GCE-GQDs-Aptamer (sequential) (9) (68 mV) < GCE-GQDs-Aptamer (premixed) (8) (262 mV) < GCE-GQDs@Aptamer (617 mV) (7). Thus there is improved charge transfer when the electrode is modified sequentially. The ΔEp for GCE-GQDs-Aptamer (sequential) (9) is near the Nernstian value. The GCE-GQDs@Aptamer (7) showing the higher ΔEp suggests bonding of the aptamer to more than one carboxylic acid functional group around the GQDs, hindering the GQDs from being able to mediate the transfer of electrons as it usually would when used to modify the electrode surface, therefore allowing for less electron transfer between the solution and electrode. The GCE-GQDs-Aptamer (premixed) (8) (262 mV) showing the second highest ΔEp in this group indicates strong adsorption of aptamer on the GQDs thus hindering the electron transfer process compared to the sequentially modified electrode with the same molecules.

For the combination of CoPc and aptamer, the reverse trend is observed where the increase in ΔEp is as follows: CoPc-Aptamer (sequential) (13, 300 mV) > CoPc-Aptamer(premixed) (12) (262 mV) > CoPc@Aptamer (166 mV) (11). This trend suggests that the CoPc may not be lying flat on the electrode surface for the covalently linked molecule (CoPc@Aptamer (11)), leaving unmodified spaces to allow for charge transfer to occur. Thus, bonded aptamer does not passivate the electrode surface, due to its orientation, as much as the aptamer on the surfaces of the premixed and sequential combinations.

In terms of current, comparing the different combinations of electrodes using GQDs and aptamer, the highest peak current was observed for GCE-GQDs-Aptamer (sequential) (9) (24.1 μA), and the lowest for GQDs-Aptamer (premixed) (8) (18.7 μA). Thus, The GCE-GQDs-Aptamer (sequential) (9) with the lowest peak separation of 68 mV has a highest a peak current of 24.1 μA which indicates better electron transfer for this electrode in this series. Comparing the combination of CoPc and aptamer, the lowest peak current was for the bonded CoPc@Aptamer (11, 14.7 μA) when compared to CoPc-Aptamer(premixed) (12, 18.2 μA) and CoPc-Aptamer(sequential) (13, 18.4 μA). The sequentially and premixed modified electrodes produce almost the same current output. It is important to note that the GCE-GQDs-CoPc(ππ)-Aptamer (premixed) (15) surface showed no peaks indicating nearly complete passivation of the electrode surface to charge transfer in the ferricyanide solution.

The modified electrodes were also tested in a buffer solution. This part of the analysis is carried out to avoid interferences from other contents within the solution once the analyte of interest is included. Fig. S2 shows the cyclic voltammograms of the electrodes in a 30 mM PBS solution of pH 7.4 without the PSA. They have been separated into three different groups for ease of comparison.

There are no peaks for bare electrode (1) in Fig. S2. As stated above the peaks labelled (I) could be due to CoIIPc−2/CoIPc−2 in CoPc containing electrodes [40], and GQDs redox process in GQDs containing electrodes [39], and the reduction of amino acids contained in the aptamer [41]. However, this peak is weak for the aptamer containing electrodes. The CoIIIPc−2/CoIIPc−2 processes are near 0 V (II). The peak at around 0.6 V (III) corresponds to Pc ring based processes as previously explained [40] and amino acids contained in the aptamer [42].

From Fig. 2, the Randles-Sevcik Eq. (1) was applied on [Fe(CN) 6]3−/4 redox couple (Fig. 2) to determine the effective electrode area.
(1)
I p
= 2.69 ×
10 5
n
3 / 2
AC
D
1 2
v
1 / 2
where Ip, A, C, D, n and v are the peak current, the effective surface area, concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4, diffusion coefficient of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4, the number of electrons involved, and scan rate, respectively. The literature value for D = 7.6 × 10−6 cm2s−1 [44] for [Fe(CN)6]3−/4 was used.

By using the effective area of the modified electrodes determined using Eq. (1) and the total charge determined by integrating the anodic peak (II) in Fig. S2, and then by employing Eq. (2) [45], surface coverages were calculated. Since the peaks observed were broad, the peak areas were integrated between a peak potential range of approximately −0.25-0.125 V.
(2)
Г =
Q nFA
where n is the number of electrons transferred (~1), F the Faraday constant (96,485C mol−1), A is the effective surface area obtained from Eq. (1) and Г is the surface coverage. The Г values are listed in Table 1. The higher surface coverages imply the increase in the electrode surface area which offers more electrocatalytic surface. The highest surface coverage is obtained for sequentially modified electrodes 10 and 14, containing the aptamer covalently linked to GQDs or CoPc. The lowest is for the electrode containing aptamer premixed with GQDs (8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge transfer using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)
ce:section.ce:para: The EIS technique was used in this work to determine the electron transfer kinetics in the presence of PSA. The Nyquist plots Fig. 3 were used to determine Rct values by fitting the Randles equivalent circuit (Fig. S3). A small Rct indicates an improved charge transfer. The impedance spectra were fitted using a Randles electronic equivalent circuit which is often used in diffusion-controlled electrochemical systems [46,47] where a well-defined semi-circle followed by a linear segment is observed. This model includes the solution resistance (Rs), the electron transfer resistance (Rct), the double-layer capacity element (Cdl) and a Warburg element (Zw) to quantify the diffusion processes of the ionic species. However, the results obtained in this work showed suppressed semi-circles and phase angles lower than 90° and thus a constant phase element (CPE) was used following the observation of similar behaviour in literature reports [47]. It is also reported that using a CPE accounts for the irregularities on the electrode surface that are a result of roughness and inhomogeneities on the modifiers [47], where the plot starts to appear more linear than the semi-circle with a defined linear segment [46]. Diffusing species were barely observed in this study as we did not use ferricyanide solution for the detection of PSA. Our methods were based on detection of PSA in PBS solution only. It is likely that the adsorption process was more dominant than the diffusion process. The idea behind this was to see the behaviour of the aptasensors without having to add a signal amplifier such as ferricyanide solution but to rely more on the electrode modifiers as signal change indicators.

The Rct values are summarized in Table 3 . The highest Rct values were observed for the bare GCE (1), GCE-GQDs (2), GCE-CoPc (3) and the GCE-GQDs-Aptamer (sequential) (9). Comparing the bare GCE (1) and the one modified with only GQDs (2), the Rct values decreases slightly, possibly due to only a slight enhancement in electron transfer. The slight increase in Rct at the GCE-GQDs-Aptamer (sequential) (9) surface may possibly be a result of how the adsorbed aptamer is assembled at the GCE surface which only causes slight increase in resistance and not a significant one as expected.

Comparing the combinations of electrodes using GQDs and aptamer only, the Rct values increase as follows: GCE-GQDs-Aptamer (sequential) (9) > GCE-GQDs@Aptamer (7) > GCE-GQDs-Aptamer (premixed) (8). Showing worse charge transfer for GCE-GQDs-Aptamer (sequential) (9) in the presence of PSA even though it showed good charge transfer in terms of ΔEp for the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution discussed above. The large Rct suggest faster passivation, hence better binding of PSA to aptamer and better detection. Thus, the electrode surface modified with GCE-GQDs-Aptamer (sequential) (9) showing poor charge transfer in the presence of PSA is an indication of good binding of PSA to the aptamer which hinders electron transfer.

For the combination of CoPc and aptamer, CoPc-Aptamer (sequential) (13) again has the largest Rct followed by CoPc-Aptamer (premixed) (12) with CoPc@Aptamer (11) being the least. Lower Rct values suggest less binding of PSA to aptamer. The orientation of the electrocatalyst on the electrode surface may play a role on how well the aptamer at the surface may be exposed to be able to bind to the PSA in solution.

The apparent charge transfer rate constant (kapp) was also calculated using Eq. (3) [48] and the results are summarized in Table 3.
(3)
k app
=
RT
F 2
A
R CT
C
where C is the concentration of the PSA, A = real surface area in cm2 and other symbols retain their usual scientific meaning. The real surface area was obtained using Eq. (1). kapp values were high where kCT were low as expected.

A plot of phase-shift against log frequency (not shown) provides further characteristic information on the frequency which cannot be obtained from the Nyquist plot. All electrodes showed the phase angle less than the ideal 90° indicative of a true capacitor (Table S2) [49]. Moreover, phase angles that are shifted towards lower frequencies (and therefore lower log(f)) in the Bode plots compared to the other electrocatalysts tested are an indication that the electrocatalyst gives better catalytic activity efficiency. The log(f) values are also summarized in Table S2. For GQDs-Aptamer combinations, the log frequency values (at the peak maximum) are in agreement with the results obtained from CV analyses where GCE-GQDs-Aptamer (sequential) (9) with a log(f) of 1.8 and a low ΔE of 68 mV, implying high charge transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration studies using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)
ce:section.ce:para: The initial stock solution of PSA with a concentration of 2 μM was diluted to concentrations ranging from 1.2–2.0 pM for the purpose of concentration studies. Electrodes with the PSA specific aptamer at the surface were selected. The chosen electrodes are listed in Table 4 . Although a number of electrodes were tested, the DPV and EIS of the best performing electrode is shown in Fig. 4 . The DPV plots of the electrode containing aptamer alone (GCE-Aptamer, 4, Fig. S4A(a)) shows two peaks near 0 V, which are known for DNA [41]. These peaks are observed on all electrodes. Peaks near zero will overlap with the CoIIIPc−2/CoIIPc−2 processes for electrodes containing CoPc. For all electrodes containing GQDs, there is a peak near −0.65 V, which as stated above is typical of GQDs [39]. There were no peaks for PSA alone as has been observed in literature [50]. DPV peaks near −0.65 V (due to GQDs) and the sharp one near 0 V were used for the determination the detection limits.

The DPV results illustrated in Fig. 4 (A) and Fig. S4 A showed that the peak currents decreased with increasing PSA concentration. Fig. 4 (B) and Fig. S4B showed an increasing in resistance to charge transfer with increasing concentration. It is reported that the immobilized aptamer undergoes a conformation change upon PSA binding such that the double stranded DNA is formed with simultaneous binding to (and covering with) PSA that decrease the charge transfer rate, between redox probe and electrode surface [51]. In other words, there is an expected increase in resistance and decrease in current as PSA concentrations increase. The calibration plots (inserts) were linear in the ranges from 1.2 to 2.0 pM (0.2 pM increments) with detection limits defined according to the equation LoD = 3σb/m, where m is the slope of the corresponding calibration curve and σb is the standard deviation of the blank [52] and 3 is a recommended factor that gives a practical confidence of 90.0% to 99.7% [53–55].

Using DPV, GCE-GQDs-Aptamer (sequential) (9), GCE-GQDs-CoPc(ππ)-Aptamer (sequential) (16) gave the lowest detection limits compared to the other electrodes tested, Table 4. Similar values were obtained in EIS analyses with the same electrodes showing better detection limits.

It is reported that patients with PSA above the cut-off value of 4 ng/mL (13.3 nM) are suspected to have prostate cancer [56,57]. Therefore, development of fast and low-cost analytical methods for early detection of prostate cancer by monitoring PSA levels is of high importance. All the tested surfaces showed detection limits below the reported value for dangerous PSA levels implying great potential for use as early prostate cancer diagnostic tools. The detection limit of the best surface tested using EIS in this work was determined as 2.51 pM and the surface was the GCE-GQDs-Aptamer (sequential) (9). The best performing surface in DPV was GCE-GQDs-CoPc(ππ)-Aptamer (sequential) (16) with LoD values of 0.66 and 0.73 pM at 0 V and − 0.65 V, respectively.

The kinetics of the developed aptasensors were also studied by observing the evolutions of the voltammograms with changing scan rate. The scan rate studies were conducted using DPV and scan rates from 0.63 mV/s to 35 mV/s. The general observation for the aptasensor is that at lower scan rates (0.63 to 15 mV/s), the peaks expected were observed in the voltammograms, showing an increasing current with increasing scan rate within this scan rate range. There were no peaks at scan rates from 20 to 35 mV/s, Fig. 5a.

The relationship between the scan rate (ν) and peak current (IP) is shown in Fig. 5(b). As an example, only the peaks marked as (III) were further analyzed. Plots were made in the scan rate range of 0.63 to 15 mV/s. The peak potential was mostly unaffected by the change in scan rate. There is a linear relationship observed between scan rate and peak current, which suggests an adsorption-controlled process [39]. However, we also illustrated the (IP) vs (√ν) plots also showed a linear relationship as well which usually indicates a diffusion-controlled process if the trendline passes through the origin. In this work, the line does not pass through the origin of the axes. This has been reported to be characteristic for the electrodic process preceded by an electrochemical reaction, followed by a homogenous chemical reaction [39]. Thus it can be concluded that the process occurring between solution and sensor are mainly adsorption controlled.

The stability, selectivity, and reproducibility of the proposed electrochemical aptasensor were also investigated by DPV using a 2 pM PSA in PBS solution of pH 7.4 (30 mM). Table 5 summarizes the LoD, repeatability and reproducibility results obtained in this work, and compared to other literature reports related to the detection of PSA [58–63]. The aptasensor reported in this work showed the lowest detection limit and one of the lowest reproducibility and repeatability relative standard deviation (RSD) % compared to some literature reports shown in Table 5. In the work reported by Heydari-Bafrooei and Shamszadeh [58], the authors used graphene oxide (GO) as part of their 3-component system which was already discussed in the introduction as being potentially unstable and not easily produced as GQDs. Moreover, AuNPs are more costly than pristine GQDs which are used in this work. Another three-component system showing higher detection limits than the one developed in this work is reported by Liu et al. [59] where AuNPs are also used and fabrication involves multiple steps whereas the fabrication process reported in this work is much simpler and shorter for the best performing aptasensor developed. Another aptasensor reported in literature and not included in the comparative Table 5 of this work is one developed by Hu and co-workers [64] where PSA was detected at femtomolar level concentrations by cleavage-based electrochemical detection using electrochemically controlled atom transfer radical polymerization -based method for signal amplification. The major drawback however, is that the fabrication process is quite time consuming and the use of gold electrodes in their method is at a cost higher than that of carbon electrodes which are used in this work. Amongst good sensitivity and low detection limits, cost effectiveness as well as ease of use are important when developing biosensors so that they may be more accessible AND user friendly to those who may need them.

The same electrode was fabricated on three different occasions and tested in the PSA solution of same concentration (2 pM). The results are illustrated in Fig. S5. There were slight differences in current output, however no changes were observed in terms of peak potential. The RSD (%) values were determined from peaks at −0.65 V and at 0 V, and were found to be 4.43% and 3.77%, respectively (Table 5). After incubation the PSA solution, the DPV scans were run for 50 cycles and the change in current response was monitored (Fig. S6). The first and the last scan were compared and it was found that the current response at the most prominent peak (0 V) decreased by only 3.9%. When the electrode was not in use, it was stored at 4 °C. After a week of storage, the final scan in the repeatability test was compared to an initial run done at the same scan rate (Fig. S7). By looking at the difference between peak currents at −0.6 V and 0 V, this electrode maintained approximately 89% of its performance ability proving to be quite stable in that time frame.

Biological samples exist as complex mixtures therefore the fabricated sensors need to be tested against potential interferences in order to validate their reliability to target a specific analyte, irrespective of what other analytes may be present in a sample. For this reason, we have chosen to take the best performing fabricated electrode in this work judging by the results obtained from EIS and DPV studies, GCE-GQDs-CoPc(ππ)-Aptamer (sequential) (9), and use the mixed solution method [65] and DPV (Fig. 6 ) to test its selectivity towards PSA in the presence of other analytes that could possibly be detected in a real sample with PSA, namely bovine serum albumin (BSA), L-cysteine and glucose. The concentration of PSA used was 2.0 pM and 2.0 nM of interferant analyte was added for non-specific binding determination. The interferants were added in access (x10−3 higher than analyte) to ensure a high enough concentration for the sensor to pick up should it be sensitive to the interferences. The fabricated electrode was incubated in the solutions as was done for PSA studies in earlier sections. Eq. (4) was used to determine values of the selectivity coefficients (Kamp) which will give a measure of the degree of interference.
(4)
K AMP
=
I MIXTURE
I PSA
− 1
PSA
INTERFERANT
where Imixture and IPSA are background corrected current responses of PSA in the presence and absence of the interferent, respectively. A Kamp value in the order of magnitude higher than 10−3 indicates strong interference and a value close to 10−3 indicates weak interference [65]. From the peaks observed at about −0.6 V (Fig. 6(I)), a Kamp values of 0.17 × 10−3, 0.08 × 10−3 and 0.04 × 10−3 were obtained for BSA, glucose and L-cysteine, respectively. At the potential close to 0 V (Fig. 6(II)) where the more prominent peak is observed, Kamp value of −0.46 × 10−3, −0.64 × 10−3 and −0.68 × 10−3 was obtained for BSA, glucose and L-cysteine, respectively. Therefore, this sensor platform can be applied without any pre-separation procedures for the determination of PSA in the presence of the tested interferents with no need for pre-treatment of sample.

The aim of the reported work was to investigate various electrode surfaces modified with different combinations of GQDs, CoPc and PSA aptamer for the detection of PSA. The novelty of this work lies in that this is the first time an MPc or GQDs are linked to an aptamer for the detection of PSA and it is the first all three (GQDs, MPc and aptamer) and employed together for sensing. Observed from the results was a decrease in current output as well as an increase in charge transfer resistance when the PSA specific aptamer was exposed to the PSA in solution. From the tested surfaces, a few of the best performing electrodes were used to determine the detection limits of the sensors. The best performing electrode was the GCE-GQDs-CoPc(ππ)-Aptamer (sequential) (16) with a detection limit of 3.66 pM determined from EIS studies and 0.66 pM and 0.73 pM determined from the two peaks observed in DPV studies. These values are below the cut-off value of 4 ng/mL (13.3 nM) of PSA detection before it can be presumed that the patient is more than likely to have prostate cancer. This electrode was then used to test the selectivity of the fabricated sensor. Selectivity coefficients (Kamp) were determined with solutions containing BSA, glucose and L-cysteine. From the peaks observed at about −0.6 V, a Kamp value of 0.17 × 10−3, 0.08 × 10−3 and 0.04 × 10−3 was obtained for BSA, glucose and L-cysteine, respectively and these values were in the order of magnitude lower than 10−3 indicating weak interference, therefore this sensor platform can be applied without any pre-separation procedures for the determination of PSA in samples containing BSA, glucose and L-cysteine. The fabricated aptasesnor also showed good stability, and reproducibility. With the above conclusions determined from the results found in the study, the fabricated aptasensor has good potential of being used as an efficient, reliable prostate cancer early detection device in real serum samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of South Africa through DSI/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) as well as Rhodes University/DST Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, South Africa.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jinorgbio.2021.111462.
